title: Hotel / Airbnb Booking — System Design
description: Design a lodging reservation system: search available stays by location and date range, then book one without ever double-booking. The themes are date-range inventory, browse-vs-book consistency (approximate search but authoritative booking), the hold-then-confirm flow, and preventing double-booking under concurrency.

nodes.label: Guest Client
nodes.description: Searches stays by date/location, holds, and books.

nodes.label: API Gateway
nodes.description: Routes search, availability, and booking requests.

nodes.label: Search Service
nodes.description: Finds properties matching location, dates, filters.

nodes.label: Search Index
nodes.description: Geo + attribute index of properties for discovery.

nodes.label: Availability Service
nodes.description: Authoritative answer to 'is this unit free for these dates?'

nodes.label: Availability Store
nodes.description: Per-unit per-date inventory; the source of truth for booking.

nodes.label: Availability Change Stream
nodes.description: Durable feed of availability transitions (held/booked/released), published via outbox/CDC committed with the truth-store write.

nodes.label: Cache & Index Projector
nodes.description: Consumes availability changes and updates the browse cache and search index.

nodes.label: Booking Service
nodes.description: Drives the hold -> confirm -> cancel state machine; enforces no-double-book.

nodes.label: Booking Store
nodes.description: Authoritative reservations and their lifecycle state.

nodes.label: Payment Service
nodes.description: Authorizes during hold, captures on confirm.

nodes.label: Hold Reaper
nodes.description: Expires holds whose checkout didn't complete, releasing dates.

functional: Search available units (listings) by location, date range, and filters.
Show a unit's calendar / availability for chosen dates.
Hold a unit's dates during checkout, then confirm a booking with payment.
Cancel a confirmed booking, free the dates, and refund per policy.
Prevent double-booking the same unit for overlapping dates.
nonFunctional: Browse is read-heavy and can be approximately fresh (fast search).
Booking must be strongly consistent: never sell the same unit twice for overlapping dates.
Low search latency; booking can be a touch slower but must be correct.
Scale to millions of units and high search volume.
Hold/confirm/cancel are retry-safe (idempotent) and self-heal abandoned checkouts.
Search and booking stay decoupled: browse traffic never touches the booking-critical store.

capacity.label: Reservable unit
capacity.note: Default unit is a listing with capacity 1/night; hotels add room_type_id with counts.

capacity.label: Availability rows
capacity.note: 1M units x 365-night horizon = 365M rows before replicas/history; ~tens of GB, shardable by unit.

capacity.label: Search QPS
capacity.note: Read-heavy browse; served from index + cache, never the truth store.

capacity.label: Hold/confirm QPS
capacity.note: ~100x less than search; write path to the truth store.

capacity.label: Stay length
capacity.note: Each hold/confirm/cancel touches N night rows, so write amplification ~3x. Room-type bookings can request R>1 rooms, multiplying count updates by R.

capacity.label: Cache invalidation
capacity.note: Each confirm/cancel emits N night changes through the projector to cache + index.

capacity.label: Hot-unit contention
capacity.note: A contested unit's date rows serialize on one shard/key — correct, but a throughput ceiling.

capacity.label: Hold TTL
capacity.note: Long enough to pay, short enough to free abandoned holds; reaped on expiry.

api.description: Search available units (listings) by location, dates, and filters (approximate availability).

Hold a unit's date range during checkout, then authorize payment. Order: reserve the range -> create hold/idempotency record -> authorize -> return hold + authId. A declined authorization releases the nights (compensation); an expired hold voids the authorization. Idempotent on Idempotency-Key.
api.response: { "holdId": "h_9", "state": "held", "expiresAt": "2026-05-20T10:10:00Z", "paymentAuthorizationId": "auth_4" }  // 409 { "error": "unavailable" } on conflict; 402 { "error": "payment_declined" } after release if auth fails

api.description: Confirm a held booking. Drives a retry-safe state machine: captures payment, finalizes inventory, persists the booking. Idempotent on Idempotency-Key. May return a transitional state while recovery is in progress.

Cancel a confirmed booking. Acceptance (confirmed -> cancelling) is the source-of-truth transition: nights are released back to open exactly once on acceptance, while the refund runs asynchronously per policy and is reconciled if it fails. Idempotent on Idempotency-Key.

dataModel.name: availability
dataModel.note: Per-unit per-date inventory — the source of truth for booking. Primary model = listing (capacity 1/night): use `state` + `hold_id`. Room-type extension: set room_type_id and use the count columns (state/hold_id are then null), reserving via `available_count >= requested_rooms`.

fields.name: room_type_id
fields.type: bigint | null (set for hotel room-type rows)

fields.name: hold_id
fields.type: uuid | null (capacity-1: the currently valid hold)

fields.name: available_count
fields.type: int | null (room-type: rooms still open this night)

dataModel.name: hold_allocations
dataModel.note: Room-type extension only: records how many rooms a hold reserved per night so release returns the exact amount (a single hold_id can't sit on a shared count). Unused for capacity-1 listings, where the night row's hold_id is enough.

dataModel.name: idempotency_keys
dataModel.note: Makes hold/confirm/cancel safe to retry — stores the request fingerprint and final response per key.

fields.name: request_hash
fields.type: varchar (detect key reuse with a different body)

technologyChoices.concern: API edge, identity, and abuse controls
tradeoff: Putting TLS termination, identity, request shaping, and per-user limits at the edge keeps search and booking services focused on domain invariants.
The edge can throttle obvious abuse, but hold/confirm idempotency still belongs in the booking service because only it can replay the original response.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed API edge with WAF, identity integration, quotas, and request logs removes most custom reverse-proxy and rate-limit plumbing for the public booking APIs.

tradeoff: A relational store is the clearest fit for per-night rows, affected-row-count checks, booking state transitions, and audit-friendly constraints.
Distributed SQL buys horizontal scale and multi-zone durability, but every strong write on hot inventory pays coordination latency.
A key-value or wide-column store can scale conditional writes, but range reservation, multi-night rollback, and reporting become application responsibilities.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed strongly consistent database with conditional writes and automated backups removes most hand-built replication, failover, and shard-management work until hot-unit contention dominates.

tradeoff: Redis/Valkey are excellent for fast TTL lookups and reaper scans, but the authoritative availability row still decides whether inventory is held.
A durable SQL or KV idempotency table is safer for replaying hold/confirm/cancel responses across process crashes.
Combining TTL hold state and idempotency in one durable store simplifies recovery, at the cost of higher write latency than an in-memory cache.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed TTL-capable KV store with conditional writes removes custom expiry indexes and much of the cleanup job bookkeeping for abandoned holds.

technologyChoices.concern: Search index and availability read cache
tradeoff: A search index handles geo and amenity filters well, while a cache answers fast approximate availability checks for requested dates.
Keeping availability in the search document is fastest for browse, but stale documents must be tolerated because booking re-checks the truth.
A separate cache makes freshness easier to tune, but each search result may need cache lookups or precomputed availability buckets.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed search service plus managed cache removes cluster sizing, shard movement, and cache failover work from the browse path.

tradeoff: A log stream fits outbox/CDC publication, replay, projector rebuilds, and multiple consumers such as cache, search, and audit.
A queue is simpler for one projector with retries and a DLQ, but weaker when search, cache, metrics, and audit all need the same ordered history.
Projectors must be idempotent because at-least-once delivery is the practical default for preserving committed availability changes.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed stream with retention, consumer lag metrics, replay, and DLQ integration removes much of the custom projector retry and offset bookkeeping.

tradeoff: A database-backed state machine is easy to reason about when every transition is explicit and locally committed.
A workflow engine makes retries, timers, compensation, and stuck-state visibility first-class, but every payment and inventory activity still needs idempotent guards.
Queues decouple slow capture/refund work from client requests, but they do not make the external payment provider transactional with local booking state.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed workflow engine with durable timers and execution history can replace ad hoc retry tables for confirm/cancel progress, while reconciliation remains necessary for external payment ambiguity.

technologyChoices.concern: Payment token, secrets, and PCI boundary
tradeoff: Keep raw card data out of the booking system; store provider tokens, authorization ids, capture ids, and refund references only.
Secrets and key management should be centralized so booking workers can call the payment provider without spreading credentials across services.
Payment references belong in durable booking state because reconciliation needs stable provider ids after process crashes and client retries.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed secrets/KMS boundary plus tokenized payment provider integration removes most in-house PCI storage scope from the hotel-booking service.

tradeoff: A simple scheduled worker is enough for hold expiry at modest scale, as long as release checks are guarded by the current hold id and state.
Workflow runtimes are stronger for long-running compensation such as void authorization, retry refund, and converge stuck confirming/cancelling states.
Kubernetes jobs or service workers give operational control, but the correctness still lives in conditional state transitions in the truth stores.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed schedulers/workflows with durable timers reduce custom cron, lease, and retry loops for reaping expired holds and reconciling stuck payment states.

technologyChoices.concern: Observability for contention, freshness, and saga health
tradeoff: Metrics should make the design's failure modes visible: conflict rate, hold expiry backlog, projector lag, cache staleness age, stuck saga count, and unmatched payment references.
Tracing is most useful across hold/confirm/cancel because correctness crosses booking, availability, payment, stream, and projector components.
Analytical stores help with long retention and dispute analysis, while hot dashboards should stay focused on SLOs and alerts.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed observability with logs, metrics, traces, and alerting removes much of the bespoke health dashboard work for browse freshness and booking correctness.

technologyChoices.concern: Audit history, retention, and dispute storage
tradeoff: Booking, cancellation, payment-reference, and availability-transition history need longer retention than abandoned holds and idempotency replay windows.
Append-only audit records make reconciliation and customer disputes easier, but they must avoid storing raw card data or unnecessary guest details.
Cold object storage is cheap for long retention; queryable analytical stores cost more but speed investigations and operational reporting.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed object storage with lifecycle policies removes most custom archival jobs for old booking histories, availability audit logs, and reconciliation evidence.

patterns.name: Date-range inventory
patterns.what: Model availability per unit per night so a stay is a contiguous range of open nights.

patterns.name: Approximate browse vs authoritative book
patterns.what: Search from a fast (slightly stale) availability cache; verify exactly at booking time.
patterns.whenToUse: Read-heavy discovery where the book step must be exact.

patterns.name: Hold (reservation lock)
patterns.what: Temporarily mark the date range held during checkout so two guests can't both pay.

patterns.name: Atomic range reservation
patterns.what: Check-and-mark the whole date range in one atomic operation (committing only if affected rows = nights) to prevent double-booking.
patterns.whenToUse: Concurrent bookings on the same unit.

patterns.name: TTL hold expiry (reaping)
patterns.what: Auto-release holds whose checkout didn't complete, returning dates to inventory.
patterns.whenToUse: Holds that must not lock inventory forever.

patterns.name: Idempotent confirm
patterns.what: An idempotency key makes confirming a booking safe to retry without double-booking/charging.
patterns.whenToUse: Money-moving confirm steps over unreliable networks.

patterns.name: Confirm saga / booking state machine
patterns.what: Model confirm as recoverable states (confirming/confirmed/confirm_failed) keyed to stable references, since external payment capture can't join a local DB transaction.
patterns.whenToUse: A confirm that crosses an external payment plus multiple stores.

patterns.name: Cancel & release
patterns.what: Cancel a confirmed booking via confirmed -> cancelling -> cancelled, releasing the nights to open exactly once, invalidating caches, and refunding per policy — idempotently.
patterns.whenToUse: Reservations that can be undone after confirmation.

patterns.name: Change stream + projector (derived stores)
patterns.what: Stream truth-store changes (durably, via an outbox/CDC committed with the write) through a projector that idempotently updates derived browse stores (cache, search index), giving freshness a named owner.
patterns.whenToUse: Approximate read views derived from an authoritative write store.

patterns.name: Reconciliation worker (stuck-state recovery)
patterns.what: A background worker scans bookings stuck in confirming/confirm_failed/cancelling and compares the local record against the payment provider's reference, finalizing, releasing, or reissuing to converge the state.
patterns.whenToUse: Money-moving sagas where a crash can leave local and provider state disagreeing.

steps.title: 1. Naive: Check Availability, Then Write a Booking (the baseline)
description: Start with the simplest booking flow: when a guest books a unit, read the availability for the dates, and if it looks free, write a booking row. Search is a query over the same availability/unit tables.

It reads naturally and is wrong under concurrency. Between the check and the write, a second guest can run the same check on the same unit, see the same dates free, and also write a booking — a classic read-then-write race that double-books the unit for overlapping dates. Separately, searching the authoritative availability tables on every browse doesn't scale to millions of units and browse-heavy traffic.

So the baseline motivates the design: pick a precise reservable unit (a listing, or a hotel room type with counts), model per-night date-range inventory, reserve a range atomically (so exactly one booking wins), and serve browse from a fast approximate index/cache while the book step verifies the truth.

view.caption: The guest client calls the API gateway and booking service, which checks and reserves dates through the availability service against the availability store.

steps.decisionPrompt: Two guests check the same unit for the same dates at the same moment, both see it free, and both write a booking. What did you just do?
whyNow: Check-then-write is the intuitive booking flow; watching it double-book under a concurrent race is exactly what motivates atomic range reservation and the approximate-browse / exact-book split.

recap.after: Bookings work by checking availability then writing a booking row; search hits the same tables.
recap.newRisk: The check-then-write gap double-books under concurrency, and searching the truth store on every browse doesn't scale.

traps.trap: Checking availability and then writing the booking in two separate steps.
traps.why: Two concurrent requests both pass the check before either writes, double-booking the unit for overlapping dates.
traps.instead: Reserve the whole date range in one atomic conditional operation so exactly one booking wins.

steps.title: 2. Model Inventory as Date-Range Availability
description: Lodging inventory is per-night: a reservable unit is available or not for each date. A stay is a contiguous range of nights, and booking it means every night in the range is open. First fix the unit of inventory. The primary model here is the Airbnb-style listing — one unit with capacity 1 per night — because it makes the core invariant a clean binary: a (unit, date) is open or not, and no-double-booking is a single state transition. Store availability per (unit, date) with a state of open/held/booked. This per-night, capacity-1 model is what makes overlapping-range conflict detection precise — two stays conflict iff they share any night.

Hotels are a deliberate extension, not a separate system: a room type ((property, room_type)) has several identical rooms, so its per-night row carries a count rather than a binary state. There the invariant changes from no-double-book to no-oversell — reserve one or more rooms from a count, sold out when the count hits zero. The schema and atomic reserve below are written for capacity 1; the room-type option spells out the counter form (available_count/held_count/booked_count and a `>= requested_rooms` condition). Individual room assignment (which physical room a guest gets) is a later operational concern, not part of the count invariant.
This availability store is the source of truth for booking; everything browse-side is derived from it.

view.caption: The guest client queries the availability service for exact PDP availability, which reads and atomically reserves date ranges in the availability store.

options.name: Per-night availability rows (default)
options.description: Availability is stored as one row per (unit, date) carrying an open/held/booked state, so a stay reserves every night row in its range.
pros: Two stays conflict iff they share any night, so overlap detection is exact and simple.
A row per (unit, date) maps cleanly onto atomic conditional updates over a date range.
Natural state machine per night (open/held/booked) for holds, reaper release, and pricing per date.
cons: A long stay touches many rows, so reserve/release operate on a range, not a single row.
Row count is units x calendar horizon (~365M for 1M units x 365 nights) — large, though bounded and shardable by unit.
Open-ended or far-future inventory needs a bounded horizon or lazy row creation.
Quantity inventory (a room type with N identical rooms) needs an available_count per night, not a single open/booked flag.

view.caption: The availability service reserves against per-night rows in the availability store, one row per date.

options.name: Interval / range records per booking
options.description: Each reservation is stored as a single (start, end) interval record, and conflicts are found with an interval-overlap query rather than per-night rows.
pros: One row per reservation (start, end) is compact — no row-per-night explosion.
Matches how a stay is conceived: a single contiguous interval.
Cheap to store for sparse, far-future inventory.
cons: Conflict detection is an interval-overlap query, which needs careful indexing to stay fast and correct.
Atomic 'reserve this range' is harder to express than a conditional update on fixed night rows.
Per-night state (held vs booked, per-date price) is awkward to attach to an interval model.

view.caption: The availability service reserves against interval records in the availability store, one range per booking.

options.name: Per-night counters (room-type / hotel extension)
options.description: For a hotel room type with N identical rooms, each (room_type, date) row carries available_count / held_count / booked_count instead of a binary state, and a reserve conditionally decrements available_count when it is >= requested_rooms.
pros: One row per (room_type, date) covers many identical rooms — no row-per-physical-room explosion.
No-oversell becomes a counter invariant: reserve succeeds only if available_count >= requested_rooms, sold out at zero.
Multiple guests can hold different rooms of the same room type concurrently — counts, not a single binary flag.
Physical room assignment can be deferred to check-in, kept out of the reservation hot path.
cons: A single hold_id can't represent which portion of the count a hold owns — needs a hold_allocations record (hold_id -> rooms) to release the right amount.
Reserve/release are conditional counter updates (decrement if >= requested, increment on release), not a clean open->held->booked transition.
Overbooking policy (deliberately selling past the physical count) is a separate decision layered on the counter.
Capacity math changes: each booking can request >1 room, so write amplification is per requested room, not just per night.

view.caption: For room types, the availability service conditionally decrements a per-night available_count in the availability store instead of flipping a binary state.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why model availability per night rather than per booking?

concepts.definition: Per-night availability; a stay reserves a contiguous range of open nights.
concepts.whyItMatters: Makes overlapping-stay conflicts exact and range reservation atomic.
concepts.example: Jun 1-4 books nights 1,2,3; conflicts with any stay touching those nights.

whyNow: The per-night inventory model is the foundation; search, holds, and double-book prevention all reason about date ranges over it.

recap.after: Per-night availability is the source of truth.
recap.newRisk: Searching this directly across millions of properties on every browse is too slow.

steps.title: 3. Search: Approximate Browse
description: Browse is read-heavy and tolerant of slight staleness. Build a search index over units (geo + attributes) and an availability read cache; search returns units that appear available for the dates, fast.

Search availability is approximate by design — a unit shown as available might get booked by someone else a second later. That's acceptable for browse; the booking step is where exactness is enforced.

Keep the derived stores fresh through an explicit path: availability transitions (held/booked/released) flow through a change stream into a projector that updates the read cache and search index — don't make every search hit the authoritative availability store, and don't leave cache freshness as an unowned side effect. Make the publication durable, not best-effort: write the change record in the same local transaction as the truth-store update (an outbox row or the store's own CDC log), so a committed availability change is guaranteed to be published even if the service crashes right after. The projector consumes at-least-once and applies updates idempotently (keyed by unit/date/version), so a redelivered event is a safe no-op rather than a corruption — the derived store reflects truth, just a little later.

view.caption: The guest client searches through the gateway and search service, which queries the search index by geo and filters and reads approximate availability from a read cache that a projector keeps current from the availability change stream.

options.name: Search index + availability read cache (default)
options.description: Browse hits a geo+attribute search index plus an availability read cache fed from booking changes, keeping the authoritative store out of the read path.
pros: Browse is fast: geo + attribute queries hit the index, approximate availability comes from the cache.
Read-heavy browse traffic never touches the authoritative availability store, which stays free for bookings.
Index and cache scale and replicate independently of the booking core.
cons: Search availability is approximate — a unit shown free may be booked a second later (corrected at the booking step).
The availability cache + index must be fed from booking changes via a change stream and projector, adding a freshness pipeline.
Two derived stores (index + cache) to keep reasonably fresh.

view.caption: The search service queries the search index and reads approximate availability from a read cache, keeping browse fast and eventually consistent.

options.name: Query the authoritative availability store directly
options.description: Search queries the authoritative availability store directly, so browse runs filters and geo lookups against the same transactional rows that bookings write.
pros: Search results are always exact — never shows a night that is actually taken.
No derived index or cache to build, feed, or keep fresh.
Simplest possible architecture: one source of truth for both browse and booking.
cons: Browse-heavy traffic hammers the booking-critical store, risking contention with real reservations.
Geo + multi-attribute filtering over millions of properties is slow on a transactional store.
Couples read scaling to the write store, the exact thing the design wants to separate.

view.caption: The search service queries the authoritative availability store directly for exact availability on every browse.

options.name: Event-driven materialized search view
options.description: Availability changes stream into a dedicated denormalized browse view that a projector keeps current, so search reads the materialized view rather than the source.
pros: Booking/availability changes stream into a dedicated browse view, so search reflects writes within seconds.
Decouples the read shape (denormalized, geo-indexed) from the write shape entirely.
Rebuildable: a corrupted view is reprojected from the authoritative store.
cons: Eventually consistent — still approximate at the moment of search, just fresher.
Adds a change stream + projector to build and operate.
More moving parts than a cache for a unit set that may not change that fast.

view.caption: Availability changes flow from the store through a change stream and projector into the search index, which the search service queries for browse.

steps.decisionPrompt: Should search read the authoritative availability store on every query?

concepts.definition: Discovery uses a fast cache; correctness is enforced only at booking.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps browse fast and cheap without risking double-booking (verified later).
concepts.example: Search shows ~available results; the hold step re-checks authoritatively.

whyNow: The browse-vs-book consistency split is the central design insight; it lets search scale while booking stays correct.

recap.after: Fast approximate search; truth reserved for booking.
recap.newRisk: Two guests can both reach checkout for the same unit — booking must not double-sell.

steps.title: 4. Hold-Then-Confirm Checkout
description: Booking is multi-step (review, pay), during which someone else mustn't grab the room. When checkout starts, the booking service runs a fixed order: reserve the date range atomically, create the hold and its idempotency record, then authorize (not capture) payment. Authorizing at hold time means the guest's card is validated and the funds are guaranteed before confirm, so confirm only has to capture.

Ordering matters because each pair of steps has a failure branch. If the atomic reserve fails, no hold or auth is created (409 unavailable). If the reserve succeeds but the authorization is declined, the booking service compensates by releasing the just-held nights, so a payment failure never strands inventory. If the hold later expires unpaid, the reaper releases the nights and the authorization is voided so the guest is not left with a lingering hold on their funds.
The hold gives the guest a window to complete payment without the dates being sold from under them. If they confirm, the hold becomes a booking; if they abandon, the hold expires.

Holds are short (minutes) so abandoned checkouts don't lock inventory — a balance between giving guests time and not starving real demand. The hold carries its own lifecycle fields — state (held/confirming/confirmed/expired), owner, payment_authorization_id, and expires_at — and each held night row records the currently valid hold_id. The reaper releases a night only when it is still held by a hold that is still expired and unconfirmed, so it never reopens nights that a newer checkout re-held or that a confirm already booked.

view.caption: The booking service reserves dates via the availability service, places a TTL hold in the hold store, then authorizes payment through the payment service.

options.name: Explicit hold with TTL (default)
options.description: Starting checkout marks the date range 'held' with a short TTL and authorizes payment; confirming turns the hold into a booking, abandonment lets it expire.
pros: Marks the nights 'held' for a few minutes so no one else can grab them during payment.
Abandoned checkouts self-heal: the hold expires and the reaper releases the nights.
Clear lifecycle (open -> held -> booked) the rest of the system can reason about.
cons: Needs a hold store and a reaper to expire abandoned holds.
Choosing the TTL is a tradeoff: too short rushes guests, too long starves real demand.
Held-but-never-confirmed inventory is briefly unsellable.

view.caption: The booking service places an explicit TTL hold in the hold store after reserving dates and before capturing payment.

options.name: No hold — reserve atomically at confirm
options.description: There is no held state — nights stay open until confirm, when the booking service atomically reserves the whole range as the final step of checkout.
pros: Simpler: no hold store, no TTL, no reaper — nights are only ever open or booked.
Inventory is never tied up by someone who is merely 'thinking about it'.
Fewer moving parts and states to operate.
cons: A guest can complete payment only to find the room was booked out from under them mid-checkout.
Wasted payment authorizations that must be voided when the final reserve fails.
Bad UX for popular rooms exactly when contention is highest.

view.caption: The booking service authorizes payment first, then atomically reserves dates in the availability store at confirm with no hold.

options.name: Pessimistic lock for the checkout window
options.description: The booking service takes a database lock on the date-range rows and keeps it for the whole payment window, blocking any other booker until checkout finishes.
pros: Holding a lock on the date range guarantees exclusivity while the guest pays.
No 'held' state to model — the lock itself blocks other bookers.
Conceptually simple exclusivity.
cons: A lock held for minutes across a slow human payment step ties up a DB resource the whole time.
A client that abandons or crashes mid-checkout strands the lock until a timeout — same expiry problem as a hold, with worse blast radius.
Long-held locks hurt throughput and can cascade under contention on hot units.

view.caption: The booking service takes a row lock on the date range in the availability store for the duration of the checkout window.

steps.decisionPrompt: A guest is on the payment screen for 3 minutes. How do you stop someone else booking the same room meanwhile?

concepts.definition: A short TTL mark on the date range during checkout so it can't be double-sold.
concepts.whyItMatters: Protects the inventory across a multi-step, human-paced checkout.
concepts.example: Mark Jun 1-4 held for 10 min; confirm -> booked; expire -> open.

whyNow: The hold is how a multi-step checkout coexists with contended inventory; it's the booking-flow backbone.

recap.after: Date range held with a TTL during payment.
recap.newRisk: Concurrent holds on the same range must not both succeed — need atomic range reservation.

traps.why: Abandoned checkouts lock rooms forever, starving real bookings.
traps.instead: Short TTL holds reaped on expiry.

steps.title: 5. Preventing Double-Booking
description: The core correctness requirement: never sell the same unit twice for overlapping dates. The hold acquisition must be atomic — check that every night in the range is open and mark them held in one operation, so two concurrent requests can't both pass the check.

Use a conditional/transactional update over the date range (or optimistic concurrency with the version column): the first request wins the range; the second sees a conflict and is rejected. Critically, the update must affect exactly N rows (one per night) — a naive `UPDATE ... WHERE date IN range AND state='open'` will happily update a partial subset if some nights are taken, so the transaction must compare the affected row count to the number of nights and roll back unless they match. The availability store, sharded by unit, serializes writes per unit.

The room-type extension uses the same one-shot discipline but as a counter, not a flip: `UPDATE availability SET available_count = available_count - :rooms, held_count = held_count + :rooms WHERE room_type AND date IN range AND available_count >= :rooms`, again requiring affected rows = nights so the whole range is reserved or none of it is, and writing a hold_allocations row per night so release returns exactly :rooms. No-oversell holds as long as every decrement is guarded by `available_count >= requested_rooms`.

This is the same check-and-reserve-atomically discipline as any limited-inventory system, applied to a contiguous date range — a binary flip for a capacity-1 listing, a guarded counter decrement for a room type.

view.caption: The booking service reserves dates through the availability service, which performs an atomic date-range reserve against the availability store to prevent double-booking.

options.name: Atomic conditional update / OCC (default)
options.description: A single conditional/transactional update checks every night is open and marks them held at once, committing only if the affected row count equals the number of nights, using a version column so the losing concurrent request just fails.
pros: Check-all-nights-open-and-mark-held in one conditional update, so two concurrent requests can't both pass.
Optimistic concurrency (version column) needs no long-held locks — the loser just retries or fails.
Sharding availability by unit serializes writes per unit naturally.
cons: Must verify affected rows = number of nights and roll back on a partial match, or a partially-taken range silently half-reserves.
The loser of a race must handle a conflict and retry, adding client-side complexity.
Hot dates on a famous unit serialize on one unit's rows — correct but a throughput ceiling.
Requires the store to support the conditional/transactional primitive over a range.

view.caption: The availability service uses an atomic conditional update (optimistic concurrency) on the availability store so only one booking wins a contested range.

options.name: Pessimistic row lock (SELECT ... FOR UPDATE)
options.description: The transaction does SELECT ... FOR UPDATE on the night rows before reserving, so the second request blocks and then sees the nights already taken.
pros: Locking the night rows before reserving guarantees exactly one writer wins, no retry loop.
Familiar transactional pattern on a relational availability store.
Deterministic: the second request blocks, then sees the nights taken.
cons: Locks held under load can queue and degrade throughput on hot units.
Risk of deadlocks when ranges are locked in different orders across requests.
Doesn't extend cleanly to a sharded/distributed store the way a conditional update does.

view.caption: The availability service takes a pessimistic SELECT ... FOR UPDATE row lock on the availability store before reserving.

options.name: Distributed lock per unit+range
options.description: A separate lock service (Redis/ZooKeeper) grants an exclusive lock on unit+range, coordinating mutual exclusion outside the availability store's own writes.
pros: A lock service (e.g. Redis/ZooKeeper) coordinates exclusivity even across multiple booking services.
Works when the availability store itself can't offer a strong conditional primitive.
Decouples mutual exclusion from the storage engine.
cons: Introduces a separate, critical dependency that must itself be HA — a new SPOF if it falls over.
Lock-then-write isn't atomic with the DB write, so a crash between them can leak a lock or a phantom reserve.
Clock/lease expiry edge cases (lock lost early) can still allow a double-book without fencing tokens.

view.caption: The booking service first acquires a unit+range lock from a distributed lock service before reserving through the availability store.

steps.decisionPrompt: Two guests submit a hold for the same unit and dates at the same millisecond. How does exactly one win?

concepts.definition: Check all nights open and mark them held in a single atomic/conditional operation.
concepts.whyItMatters: Prevents the read-then-write race that causes double-booking.
concepts.example: UPDATE availability SET state='held' WHERE unit=u AND date IN range AND state='open' — commit only if affected rows = number of nights, else roll back.

whyNow: Double-book prevention is the non-negotiable correctness property; atomic check-and-mark is the precise mechanism and the senior signal.

recap.after: Exactly one hold wins a contested range.
recap.newRisk: The confirm step (payment) can be retried or interrupted — it must be idempotent.

traps.trap: Check availability, then mark held in a separate step.
traps.why: Two requests both read 'open' before either marks held -> double-booking.
traps.instead: Atomic conditional update over the whole range.

flows.name: Atomic range reservation: exactly one hold wins
flows.note: Two concurrent holds hit the same range; the conditional update over all nights succeeds for exactly one, the other sees a conflict.

steps.title: 6. Confirm, Idempotency, and Expiry
description: Confirm crosses an external payment capture and two stores (availability + bookings), so it cannot be one atomic transaction. Model it as a retry-safe state machine (a small saga): booking goes confirming -> confirmed (or confirm_failed), each step keyed to stable references (hold_id, payment_authorization_id) and guarded by an idempotency key so a retried confirm never creates two bookings or double-charges. 'Atomic' applies only to the local availability update (held nights -> booked), not to the payment leg.

The recoverable edges are the whole point. Capture-succeeded-but-write-failed: a retry recognizes the existing capture (via the authorization id / idempotency record) and finalizes exactly one booking. Write-succeeded-but-response-lost: a retry replays the stored response instead of redoing work. Hold-expiry racing confirm: confirm first transitions the hold held -> confirming (CAS), so the reaper, which only releases still-held expired holds, can no longer reopen the nights mid-confirm.
If the guest abandons checkout, the reaper expires the hold past its TTL and releases the nights back to 'open' — inventory self-heals without manual cleanup.

Cancellation is a separate lifecycle edge, not the inverse of confirm: confirmed -> cancelling -> cancelled, releasing the booked nights to open exactly once, emitting a cache/search invalidation, and refunding per policy — all idempotent. (See the Cancel API for the flow.) Ordering is a deliberate choice: cancellation acceptance (confirmed -> cancelling) is the source-of-truth transition, and the nights are released the moment the booking enters cancelling, while the refund runs asynchronously and is reconciled by policy. This maximizes inventory reuse — the room goes back on sale immediately — at the cost of a possible support case if a refund later fails, which the reconciliation loop surfaces. (Release-after-refund is the stricter alternative; it keeps inventory unavailable longer but never releases a room whose money hasn't been returned. The release-on-acceptance choice here favors availability.) Confirm and cancel are the money-moving steps, so correctness here mirrors the wallet/payment discipline.

Stuck money-moving states need an owner. A reconciliation worker periodically scans bookings stuck in confirming, confirm_failed, or cancelling past a threshold and compares the local record against the payment provider's reference (the authorization/capture/refund id): capture present but booking unwritten -> finalize; capture absent on a confirm_failed -> release the hold; refund unissued on a cancelled -> reissue. This closes the gap where a process dies between a payment call and the local write.

view.caption: The booking service captures payment and persists the reservation to the booking store, while a hold reaper finds expired holds and releases their dates back to the availability store.

steps.decisionPrompt: The guest pays, but the confirm response is lost and they retry. How do you avoid a second booking/charge?

concepts.term: Confirm as a retry-safe state machine (saga)
concepts.definition: Booking lifecycle states (confirming/confirmed/confirm_failed/cancelling/cancelled) with each step keyed to stable references and an idempotency key, instead of an impossible atomic payment+DB transaction.
concepts.whyItMatters: External capture can't join a local transaction; explicit states + idempotency make every interrupted step recoverable and replayable.
concepts.example: Crash after capture, before write -> retry sees the capture and finalizes one booking; lost response -> retry replays the stored result.

concepts.definition: A reaper releases nights of still-held, still-expired, unconfirmed holds back to open.
concepts.whyItMatters: Abandoned holds never permanently lock inventory, and the still-held guard avoids racing a confirm or a newer hold.
concepts.example: An unpaid hold passes its TTL; the reaper sets its nights open only if they're still held by that hold.

whyNow: A retry-safe confirm state machine + reaping + a real cancel path complete the correctness story: no double-booking, no double-charge, no leaked inventory, and a clean release on cancellation.

recap.before: Confirm was described as one atomic payment+DB step; cancellation was prose-only; holds could leak.
recap.after: Confirm is a retry-safe saga; cancel releases nights once and refunds per policy; expired holds auto-released.
recap.newRisk: Hot units (popular listing, event dates) concentrate contention; search freshness lags truth.

failureDrills.scenario: Booking service crashes after capturing payment but before recording the booking.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Retry with the idempotency key recognizes the captured payment (via the authorization id) and finalizes exactly one booking.
failureDrills.mitigation: Confirm saga keyed to hold/payment references; payment capture is checked before re-capturing.

failureDrills.scenario: Confirm succeeds but the response is lost; the client retries.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The idempotency record is found in 'succeeded' state and its stored response is replayed — no second booking or charge.
failureDrills.mitigation: Idempotency-Key record stores request hash, status, and final response.

failureDrills.scenario: The hold's TTL expires at the same moment the guest hits confirm.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Confirm CAS-transitions the hold held -> confirming first; the reaper, which only releases still-held expired holds, skips it. If the reaper won the CAS, confirm sees the hold gone and returns hold_expired.
failureDrills.mitigation: Single held -> confirming transition guards confirm vs reaper.

failureDrills.scenario: Payment capture is permanently declined at confirm.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Booking moves to confirm_failed and the held nights are released back to open; the client is told to retry payment.
failureDrills.mitigation: Saga compensation releases the hold on a terminal capture failure.

failureDrills.scenario: A booking is stuck in 'confirming' (or 'cancelling') after the process died between a payment call and the local write, with no client retry.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The reconciliation worker picks it up past a threshold, checks the payment provider's reference, and converges it: finalize if captured, release if not; reissue a missing refund on a cancelled booking.
failureDrills.mitigation: Reconciliation worker scans stuck saga states and compares local records against payment-provider references.

flows.name: Confirm saga: capture, finalize, persist — once
flows.note: Confirm CAS-locks the hold (held -> confirming), captures the authorized payment, marks held nights booked, persists the booking, and records the result under the idempotency key. A retry for the same key replays the stored response.

flows.name: Recovery: capture succeeded but write failed
flows.note: The booking service crashed after capturing payment but before persisting the booking. The retried confirm detects the existing capture and the in_progress idempotency record and finalizes exactly one booking instead of re-capturing.

steps.title: 7. Scaling Search, Hot Properties, and Freshness
description: Shard the availability and booking stores by unit id so per-unit writes serialize locally and load spreads. Search scales separately: the index + availability cache are derived and replicated for browse volume (~50k search QPS vs ~500 hold/confirm QPS, so the two tiers size very differently).

Hot dates/units (a festival weekend, a famous listing) concentrate booking contention on one unit's rows; this is bounded — contention is per-unit, and the atomic range reservation keeps it correct, just serialized. For a single famous unit that is a real write hotspot, admission control (a short waiting room/queue in front of its hold endpoint) smooths the spike without breaking correctness.

Keep search freshness reasonable by streaming availability changes through the projector into the cache and index (a booking removes those nights from browse within seconds). Stale search is acceptable because booking re-checks the truth — the cache only needs to be approximately right.

Operate it with signals, not vibes: conflict rate on contested ranges, hold-expiry/reaper backlog, projector lag / outbox backlog (the health of the search-freshness pipeline — if it grows, browse is drifting from truth), cache staleness age, confirm-saga stuck count (bookings stuck in confirming/cancelling), unmatched-payment-reference count, and search-to-hold conversion. A reconciliation worker closes the loop on stuck saga states, comparing local records against payment-provider references. Keep an availability audit/history of state transitions for reconciliation and disputes.

Mind data retention and privacy: guest IDs and payment references are sensitive, so store payment references (not raw card data — that stays with the payment provider behind a token), set TTLs on idempotency-key records once their replay window has passed, and define retention for booking history and the availability audit log per legal/financial requirements (financial records often must be kept for years, while abandoned-hold and idempotency rows can be pruned quickly). Access to guest and payment fields is least-privilege and audited.

view.caption: The search service reads approximate availability from a cache the projector refreshes from the availability change stream, keeping browse fast while bookings persist to the booking store.

steps.decisionPrompt: A famous listing's peak weekend gets thousands of concurrent booking attempts. What stays correct, and what's the bottleneck?
whyNow: Closing on sharding + hot units + cache freshness + operational signals shows the browse/book split scales and stays correct under contention.

recap.before: Single stores; unbounded freshness; no operational signals.
recap.after: Unit-sharded truth; replicated approximate search; cache updated via a durable (outbox/CDC) change stream + idempotent projector; a reconciliation worker converges stuck sagas; metrics on contention, projector lag, freshness, and saga health.
recap.newRisk: Search-vs-truth lag and per-unit hot rows — bounded by re-check-at-book and per-unit serialization.

bottlenecks.mitigation: Per-unit sharding serializes its writes; atomic range reservation keeps correctness; admission control (waiting room) smooths a single famous unit's spike; show 'just booked' fast via cache update. Watch the conflict rate on contested ranges.

Stream booking changes durably (outbox/CDC committed with the write) through the projector to the cache/index; the book step re-verifies authoritatively. Watch cache staleness age and projector lag / outbox backlog — a growing backlog is the early warning that browse is drifting from truth.

bottlenecks.issue: Confirm/cancel sagas stuck or holds leaking.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Alert on bookings stuck in 'confirming'/'cancelling', reaper backlog, and unmatched-payment-reference counts; a reconciliation worker compares stuck states against payment-provider references and the availability audit/history to converge them.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Lodging Reservation System
finalDesign.description: The reservable unit is a listing (capacity 1/night, a binary open/held/booked invariant) or — as an extension — a hotel room type with per-night counts (a no-oversell counter invariant); availability is modeled per unit per night (the source of truth). Browse is served from a fast, approximate search index + availability cache, kept current by a durable change stream (outbox/CDC committed with the truth-store write) and an idempotent projector. The booking flow enforces exactness: starting checkout places a TTL hold via an atomic range reservation (check-all-nights-open-and-mark-held in one operation, committing only if affected rows = nights), so concurrent attempts can't double-book; payment is authorized at hold (released/voided if the auth fails or the hold expires) and captured on confirm. Confirm is a retry-safe state machine (confirming -> confirmed/confirm_failed) keyed to hold/payment references and an idempotency key, not an atomic payment+DB transaction. A reaper releases abandoned, still-held holds; a reconciliation worker converges sagas stuck in confirming/cancelling against payment-provider references. Cancellation runs confirmed -> cancelling -> cancelled, releasing nights once on acceptance with the refund handled asynchronously, and invalidating caches. Availability/booking stores shard by unit (serializing per-unit writes); the book step always re-checks the truth.

view.caption: The guest client reaches the gateway for search over the index and availability cache (kept fresh by a change stream + projector), and booking, where the booking service reserves via the availability store, places holds, pays, persists to the booking store, and releases dates on cancel, while a reaper releases expired holds.

functional.how: TTL hold + retry-safe confirm saga with payment.
confirmed -> cancelling -> cancelled releases nights once, invalidates caches, and refunds per policy (idempotent cancel API).

nonFunctional.how: Replicated search index + availability cache kept fresh by a change stream + projector.
Atomic check-and-mark per-unit (commit only if affected rows = nights).
TTL holds + retry-safe confirm saga + reaper.
Unit-sharded stores + separate search tier fed by a change stream + projector.

say: Pin the reservable unit first: a listing (capacity 1/night) vs a hotel room type with counts — no-double-book is only meaningful at that key.
Confirm date-range inventory and the browse(approx)-vs-book(exact) split.
Pin no-double-booking as the hard correctness requirement; note browse is read-heavy, booking comparatively rare but exact.
Per-night availability as the source of truth, keyed by the chosen unit.
Fast approximate search from an index + cache, kept fresh by a change stream + projector.
Atomic range reservation (commit only if affected rows = nights) to prevent double-booking.
Confirm as a retry-safe saga (capture/write/response recovery) + hold reaping with a still-held guard.
Cancellation: confirmed -> cancelling -> cancelled, release once, invalidate caches, refund per policy.
Unit sharding, hot dates, and search freshness.
Tradeoffs: hold TTL length, search staleness vs truth, per-unit contention.
Operational signals: conflict rate, reaper backlog, stuck confirm sagas, cache staleness age.
Mention overbooking strategies and quantity inventory (rooms > 1/night) as extensions.

expectations: Models per-night availability and books it.
May miss the atomic reservation race, the reservable-unit choice, and the browse/book split.
Names the reservable unit explicitly and designs approximate browse vs exact book.
Atomic range reservation (with the rows = nights check) + TTL holds.
Confirm as a retry-safe saga + reaper; a real cancel/release path.
Reasons about per-unit contention, sharding, and a named change-stream/projector for derived freshness.
Handles confirm recovery (capture/write/response) and hold-vs-confirm-vs-reaper races precisely.
Discusses operational signals, quantity inventory, overbooking policy, and hold-TTL tradeoffs.

followUps: What is your reservable unit — a listing, a room, or a room type with counts — and how does that choice change the schema?
Walk through confirm recovery: payment captured but the booking write failed, then the client retries. What happens?
How does cancellation release nights exactly once and keep search/caches consistent?
How would you intentionally overbook (like airlines) and manage the risk?
How do you keep search availability fresh without hammering the truth store?
How long should a hold TTL be, and what's the tradeoff?

Switch from single-unit listings to hotel room types with quantity: how do the schema, atomic reserve, and release change — and how do you layer in individual room assignment and an overbooking policy?
How do you prevent a hot listing's peak dates from becoming a write hotspot?
